close_reason: Plugin + server endpoints implemented, 219 tests green, v2026.03.9 tagged and pushed
description:
## Context
Build a Claude Code plugin for open-brain that auto-captures learnings from subagents and sessions — like claude-mem does, but backed by open-brain's Postgres+pgvector server.

## What the Plugin Bundles
- **Hooks**: PostToolUse (capture observations), Stop (session summary), SessionStart (inject context)
- **Skills**: Memory search (3-layer: search → timeline → get_observations)
- **Scripts**: Worker/client that talks to remote open-brain server via HTTP
- **plugin.json**: Metadata, dependencies, version

## Key Design Decisions
- Remote server model (HTTP to open-brain), NOT local SQLite — enables shared knowledge across users/projects
- Memory types: `observation` (raw auto-capture), `session_summary` (end-of-session), `learning` (refined/triaged)
- Deduplication via content hash (short window) to avoid spamming from PostToolUse
- SessionStart injects recent relevant memories into context

## Reference
- claude-mem plugin v10.5.2 at ~/.claude/plugins/cache/thedotmack/claude-mem/
- claude-mem hooks: PostToolUse (every tool), Stop (summarize), SessionStart (inject)
- claude-mem worker: SQLite + ChromaDB local — we replace with HTTP to open-brain server

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Plugin package created (plugin.json, hooks.json, skills, scripts)
- [ ] PostToolUse hook captures observations and sends to open-brain server
- [ ] Stop hook generates session summary and saves to open-brain
- [ ] SessionStart hook injects recent memories into context
- [ ] Installable via Claude Code plugin mechanism
- [ ] Documentation for setup (server URL, auth credentials)

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | Plugin package | review | plugin.json + hooks.json + skills exist |
| 2 | PostToolUse capture | integ | Manual test: tool use → observation in open-brain |
| 3 | Stop summary | integ | Manual test: session end → summary saved |
| 4 | SessionStart injection | integ | Manual test: new session shows recent memories |
| 5 | Installable | demo | Install in fresh Claude Code instance |
| 6 | Docs | doc | README with setup instructions |
title: Create open-brain Claude Code plugin (like claude-mem)

close_reason: session_ref param + upsert logic in save_memory; ALTER TABLE IF NOT EXISTS on startup; 140/140 tests passing; SKILL.md already had correct session_ref usage
description:
## Problem
Session summaries are the #1 source of memory bloat. Multiple summaries per session/bead get created instead of updating existing ones.

## Solution
1. Add `session_ref` field to save_memory (optional, for session summaries)
   - Accepts bead ID (e.g. beads-abc) or free-text session name
2. When saving a memory with type=session_summary and a session_ref:
   - Check if a memory with same session_ref already exists
   - If yes: UPDATE (merge content) instead of INSERT
   - If no: INSERT as normal
3. Update /session-close skill instructions in CLAUDE.md to always pass bead ID or session name when saving session summaries

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] save_memory accepts optional session_ref parameter
- [ ] Upsert logic prevents duplicate session summaries for same ref
- [ ] /session-close skill docs updated to use session_ref

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | session_ref param | unit | test_save_memory_session_ref() |
| 2 | Upsert logic | integ | test_session_summary_upsert() |
| 3 | Skill docs | doc | CLAUDE.md diff |
title: Session summary dedup: session_ref field + upsert logic

close_reason: Epic geplant: 8 Child-Beads erstellt (5m7→3t3/4fn/oyp→4rl/8j2/72i/wt7), Abhängigkeitsgraph gesetzt. Pipeline-Fundament: save_memory/update_memory/search metadata (5m7) ist jetzt entblockt.
description:
## Ziel
Die gesamte Learnings-Pipeline (Extract → Review → Materialize) läuft vollständig über open-brain statt JSONL.

## Hintergrund
learnings.jsonl war der bisherige Single Point of Truth für Learnings. open-brain übernimmt diese Rolle: save_memory für Extract, ob-triage für Review/Materialize.

## Kernproblem
Das metadata-JSON-Feld existiert bereits in der DB, wird aber noch nicht über die MCP-Tools geschrieben. Das ist der einzige echte Gap — alles andere baut darauf auf.

## Child Beads (Implementierungsreihenfolge)
1. save_memory/update_memory/search: metadata-Parameter (Fundament)
2. migrate_learnings.py neu schreiben (vollständiges Feldmapping)
3. learning-extractor Agent: JSONL → save_memory
4. learnings-pipeline Skill: Extract-Phase aktualisieren
5. ob-triage: Status-Tracking nach Promote/Discard
6. ob-triage SKILL.md: Learning Lifecycle dokumentieren
7. learnings-pipeline Skill: auf Redirect-Stub kürzen
8. learning-extractor agent.yml: cleanup

## Abhängigkeitsgraph
Bead-A (metadata) → Bead-B (migration)
Bead-A → Bead-C (extractor) → Bead-D (pipeline skill) → Bead-G (redirect)
Bead-A → Bead-E (ob-triage status) → Bead-F (ob-triage docs)
Bead-C → Bead-H (agent cleanup)
title: Epic: Learning Pipeline vollständig auf open-brain migrieren

close_reason: Research abgeschlossen: Plugin (marketplace.json + plugin/), Docker Compose, .env.example bereits implementiert. Entscheidung: self-hosted Docker + Claude Code Marketplace, kein SaaS. Follow-up: open-brain-2tm (ghcr.io publish)
description:
## Context
open-brain currently requires:
1. A running server (Python + Postgres + pgvector)
2. A Claude Code plugin (installed via --plugin-dir)

claude-mem is distributed as a single Claude Code plugin (via marketplace) with local SQLite+ChromaDB.
open-brain is architecturally different: shared remote server + thin plugin client.

## Research Questions
- [ ] How does Claude Code plugin marketplace distribution work? (npm? GitHub? registry?)
- [ ] Can the plugin be published standalone (users point at their own server)?
- [ ] What's the best packaging for the server? Docker Compose? Helm chart? 1-click deploy?
- [ ] Should open-brain ship as a Docker image on ghcr.io/dockerhub?
- [ ] How to handle the Postgres+pgvector dependency? (managed DB vs bundled?)
- [ ] What's the minimum viable setup for a new user? (docker compose up + plugin install?)
- [ ] Should we offer a hosted/SaaS option? (multi-tenant open-brain server)
- [ ] How does open-brain-cot (publishable: Docker Compose, README, .env.example) relate?
- [ ] Review claude-mem marketplace listing for distribution patterns

## Deliverable
Research findings + decision on distribution strategy.
Follow-up beads for implementation.

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | Research complete | doc | Findings in bead notes |
| 2 | Distribution decision | doc | Decision with rationale |
| 3 | Follow-up beads | review | Linked implementation beads |
notes:
Context: open-brain-cot (Docker Compose, README, .env.example) is already closed. This research is for the NEXT iteration: public distribution (marketplace, hosted option, user onboarding). Not urgent.
## Research Findings (2026-03-10)

### Was bereits implementiert ist
- Plugin: `plugin/` Verzeichnis mit Hooks (PostToolUse, SessionStart, Stop/SubagentStop), Skills (ob-search), README
- Marketplace: `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` für Claude Code Plugin Distribution (claude --plugin-dir + marketplace registry)
- Server-Packaging: `Dockerfile` + `docker-compose.yml` (gebündeltes Postgres+pgvector + App), `.env.example`
- README Quick Start via Docker Compose bereits dokumentiert

### Offene Punkte
1. Docker Image noch nicht auf ghcr.io/Docker Hub veröffentlicht (README nutzt noch `build: .` statt `image: ghcr.io/...`)
2. README GitHub URL noch Placeholder (`github.com/your-org/open-brain`) 
3. Hosted/SaaS Option: nicht geplant (too complex, wrong fit für personal-use tool)
4. Plugin Marketplace Submission: marketplace.json existiert, aber kein GitHub Actions Release-Workflow

### Verteilungs-Entscheidung
- **Plugin**: Claude Code Marketplace via marketplace.json (self-serve: `claude --plugin-dir` oder zukünftig `claude plugin install open-brain`)
- **Server**: Self-hosted Docker Compose (Postgres gebündelt) – Hosted/SaaS NICHT angeboten (Komplexität/Kosten/Privacy)
- **Docker Image**: Publish auf ghcr.io via GitHub Actions für einfacheres `docker compose pull` ohne lokales Build
- **Multi-tenant**: Nicht geplant, single-tenant self-hosted bleibt der primäre Use Case
title: Research: publish open-brain as installable package (server + plugin)

close_reason: Python MCP server ported: 8 tools, FastMCP + asyncpg + PyJWT, 127 unit tests, 82% coverage
description:
open-brain MCP Server von TypeScript nach Python portieren via TDD.

## Scope
- Test-Spec aus bestehendem TS-Code ableiten (8 Tools, DataLayer, Auth)
- Python-Projekt scaffolden (FastAPI/Starlette, asyncpg, mcp SDK)
- JWT-Validation gegen Keycloak (PyJWT + JWKS)
- TDD: Tests first, dann Implementierung
- SQL-Migrations bleiben identisch (bereits in Postgres)

## Tools zu portieren
1. search (hybrid: vector + FTS via RRF)
2. timeline (anchor-based context window)
3. get_observations (bulk fetch by ID)
4. save_memory (insert + async embedding + auto-linking)
5. search_by_concept (pure vector search)
6. get_context (recent sessions)
7. stats (DB aggregates)
8. refine_memories (LLM-powered consolidation)

## Akzeptanzkriterien
1. Alle 8 Tools funktional äquivalent zum TS-Code
2. JWT-Validation gegen Keycloak funktioniert
3. Hybrid Search (pgvector + FTS + RRF) liefert gleiche Ergebnisse
4. Voyage-4 Embedding Integration funktioniert
5. Test-Suite mit >80% Coverage
6. TS-Code kann entfernt werden

## Means of Compliance

| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|-----|-----|----------|
| 1 | 8 Tools funktional | integ | test_tools.py |
| 2 | JWT/Keycloak Auth | integ | test_auth.py |
| 3 | Hybrid Search | unit | test_search.py |
| 4 | Voyage Embedding | integ | test_embedding.py |
| 5 | Coverage >80% | unit | pytest --cov Report |
| 6 | TS entfernt | review | git diff |

## Dependencies
- Keycloak LXC muss laufen (elysium-tfy)
title: Python MCP Server: TDD Port von TypeScript

description:
## Kontext
Das aktuelle migrate_learnings.py verliert status, extracted_at, affected_skills, materialized_to und andere Felder. Neuschreiben mit vollständigem Feldmapping.

## Mapping JSONL → open-brain
- content → text (content-Feld)
- id → session_ref als 'lrn:<id>' (Idempotenz)
- extracted_at → created_at im DB-INSERT (direkter asyncpg-Insert)
- source.project → project
- metadata-JSON: {status, confidence, feedback_type, scope, affected_skills, content_hash, extracted_at, materialized_to, discard_reason, source}
- Status-Mapping: open→open, materialized→materialized, discarded→discarded, unknown→open
- Alle 367 Einträge importieren (nicht nur open — discarded/materialized als historischer Kontext)

## Akzeptanzkriterien
- [ ] Alle JSONL-Felder in metadata-JSON übertragen
- [ ] Idempotenz: Re-Run ohne Duplikate
- [ ] Verify-Query nach Import zeigt korrekte Verteilung
- [ ] --dry-run Modus funktioniert

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vollständiges Mapping | unit | test_full_metadata_mapping |
| 2 | Idempotenz | unit | test_import_idempotent |
| 3 | Verify-Query | doc | Output des Verify-Runs im Close-Reason |

## Relevante Dateien

title: [open-brain] migrate_learnings.py: vollständiges Metadata-Mapping

close_reason: ob-triage SKILL.md implemented with full HITL convention (keep/merge/archive/delete/promote/scaffold). AskUserQuestion integrated for structured decisions. Plugin v0.2.0 deployed. delete via REST API documented.
description:
## Context
open-brain-pvv hat die MCP-Tools `triage_memories` + `materialize_memories` + `run_lifecycle_pipeline` gebaut.
Dieser Bead implementiert den **ob-triage Plugin-Skill** mit HITL-Konvention.

## Was gebaut wird

### ob-triage Skill (Plugin, projektübergreifend)
- Ruft triage_memories (dry_run) auf, lädt volle Memory-Texte
- Präsentiert Ergebnisse nach HITL-Konvention (siehe unten)
- User bestätigt en-bloc oder einzeln, dann Ausführung

## HITL-Konvention

### Keep — kein Approval
Kurze Auflistung mit Titeln.

### Merge — Details zeigen
En-bloc: Paare mit Grund, "alle mergen oder einzeln?"
Einzeln: Beide Source-Texte + vorgeschlagener Merge-Text. User: annehmen/ablehnen/ändern.

### Archive vs. Delete — unterscheiden
- Archive: Prio auf 0.1, bleibt durchsuchbar
- Delete: Wirklich entfernen
Beides mit Grund, en-bloc oder einzeln.

### Promote — immer einzeln HITL, Ziel-Auswahl
Ziele: standards/ | CLAUDE.md (projekt/global) | Skill | Bead (scaffold)
Memory-Text + Zielvorschlag zeigen, User entscheidet.

### Scaffold — immer einzeln HITL
Bead-Titel + Beschreibung, User bestätigt vor bd create.

## Triage-Kategorien
keep | merge | archive | delete | promote (standard/skill/claude-md/code) | scaffold

## Akzeptanzkriterien
- [ ] Plugin-Skill ob-triage existiert und ist aus jedem Projekt aufrufbar
- [ ] Skill ruft triage_memories auf und präsentiert nach HITL-Konvention
- [ ] En-bloc und Einzel-Review möglich
- [ ] Promote zeigt Ziel-Auswahl
- [ ] Archive vs. Delete unterscheidbar
- [ ] Merge zeigt beide Sources + Merge-Text

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | Skill existiert | demo | Skill aus fremdem Projekt aufrufen |
| 2 | HITL-Präsentation | demo | Interaktive Triage-Session |
| 3 | En-bloc + Einzel | demo | Beide Modi zeigen |
| 4 | Promote Ziel-Auswahl | demo | Promote mit Ziel |
| 5 | Archive vs Delete | demo | Beide Aktionen |
| 6 | Merge mit Details | demo | Merge mit Source-Texten |

## Ausgelagert
- JSONL-Migration + learnings-pipeline Umbau → open-brain-ddz
notes: Step 1 done: ob-triage SKILL.md created at plugin/skills/ob-triage/SKILL.md with full HITL convention (keep/merge/archive/delete/promote/scaffold). Next: test in a real triage session, then tackle JSONL migration + learnings-pipeline umbau.
title: Triage Pipeline: Learnings Review + Materialisierung als Plugin-Skills

close_reason: Parallelisierung via Waves + skip_llm_merge bei Similarity>=0.92 und low-priority Scope, 225 Tests green
description:
## Problem
refine_memories is slow because each merge-Action runs sequentially (1 LLM-Call + 1 Embedding-Call per merge). With 80 merges from 500 candidates, that's 80 sequential API calls.

## Changes
1. Parallelize independent merge-Actions via asyncio.gather() (with conflict detection for overlapping IDs)
2. Merge-LLM only when needed:
   - duplicates scope: skip LLM merge when similarity >= 0.92 (keep better memory)
   - low-priority scope: never LLM merge (not worth it)
   - recent/project scopes: always LLM merge
3. Pass similarity info through to _execute_refine_action

## Means of Compliance

| # | Akzeptanzkriterium | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|-------------------|-----|----------|
| 1 | Merges run in parallel (no sequential bottleneck) | review | Code review |
| 2 | duplicates scope skips LLM merge at similarity >= 0.92 | unit | test |
| 3 | low-priority scope never calls merge LLM | unit | test |
| 4 | recent/project scopes still use LLM merge | review | Code review |
| 5 | No functional regression (merges still work) | unit | existing tests pass |
title: Optimize refine_memories: parallelize merges + smart merge-LLM

close_reason: Phase 1 done: /api/session-capture Endpoint deployed, Hook-Ansatz evaluiert und zugunsten Instruction-Ansatz verworfen. Cursor separat nicht nötig (MCP). Folge-Bead claude-5vo für Agent Instructions.
description:
Automatisches Erfassen von Observations aus Konversationen — inspiriert von Nex's Auto-Capture Pattern.

## Kontext

Nex (nex-crm/nex-as-a-skill) löst das Problem, dass Memory-Stores nur bei explizitem `store`-Aufruf befüllt werden. Stattdessen: Hooks die nach Sessions automatisch relevante Fakten extrahieren und in open-brain speichern.

## Scope

### Phase 1: Claude Code Hook (Stop-Event)
- Hook auf `Stop` Event in Claude Code
- LLM extrahiert speicherwürdige Observations aus der Konversation
- Deduplizierung gegen bestehende Memories (open-brain hat `refine_memories` bereits)
- Rate-Limiting: nicht jede kurze Antwort triggert Capture

### Phase 2: Cursor Integration
- Prüfen ob Cursor Hook-Support hat (MCP ja, Hooks unklar)
- Falls ja: gleiches Pattern wie Claude Code
- Falls nein: SKILL.md-basierter Fallback

### Phase 3: shikigami-bot Middleware
- Bot-Middleware: nach Konversationsende relevante Fakten extrahieren
- open-brain MCP-Client im Bot integrieren

## Design-Entscheidungen (offen)
- Was ist "speicherwürdig"? LLM-basierte Filterung vs. Heuristiken
- Welches LLM für Extraction? Haiku 4.5 (günstig) vs. Sonnet (besser)
- Opt-in oder Opt-out pro Session?

## Akzeptanzkriterien
1. Claude Code Hook existiert und triggert auf Stop-Event
2. Hook extrahiert Observations und speichert sie via open-brain MCP
3. Deduplizierung verhindert doppelte Einträge
4. Rate-Limiting: kurze Sessions (<3 Nachrichten) werden übersprungen
5. Cursor-Kompatibilität geprüft und dokumentiert
6. shikigami-bot Middleware-Konzept dokumentiert

## Means of Compliance

| # | Akzeptanzkriterium | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|-------------------|-----|----------|
| 1 | Hook existiert und triggert | e2e | Manueller Test mit Claude Code Session |
| 2 | Observations werden gespeichert | integ | open-brain search nach auto-capture |
| 3 | Deduplizierung funktioniert | unit | Test mit doppelten Observations |
| 4 | Rate-Limiting greift | unit | Test mit kurzer Session |
| 5 | Cursor-Kompatibilität | doc | Dokumentation in CLAUDE.md oder Bead-Notes |
| 6 | shikigami-bot Konzept | doc | Design-Dokument in Bead-Notes |
notes:
## Phase 1: Claude Code Hook — DONE

**Server**: `/api/session-capture` endpoint added to open-brain (server.py)
- Accepts conversation text + session metadata
- Haiku extracts 1-5 observations + session summary
- Rate limiting: skips < 3 user messages
- Truncation: 8k char limit for Haiku context

**Hook**: `malte/hooks/auto-capture.py` in claude repo
- Stop hook reads transcript_path from stdin JSON
- Parses JSONL, extracts user/assistant text
- Filters system messages (reminders, tool results, bash output)
- Sends to open-brain via x-api-key auth
- Loop prevention via stop_hook_active flag

**Registered**: in ~/.claude/settings.json Stop hooks

**Verified**: E2E test with real transcript → 5 observations + summary saved

## Phase 2: Cursor — TODO
- Need to verify if Cursor supports Stop hooks or equivalent
- MCP connection to open-brain already works

## Phase 3: shikigami-bot — TODO
- Bot middleware concept needed
- Could use same /api/session-capture endpoint
## Design-Entscheidung: Hook-Ansatz verworfen zugunsten Instruction-Ansatz

**Verworfen**: Stop-Hook (auto-capture.py) — feuert jeden Turn, nicht deterministisch, braucht Debouncing
**Gewählt**: Agent Closing Instructions — Agents reflektieren selbst und rufen save_memory auf

### Architektur
- **Smart Clients** (Claude Code, Cursor, Agents): `save_memory` via MCP — Agent reflektiert selbst
- **Dumb Clients** (shikigami-bot, Scripts): `/api/session-capture` via REST — Haiku reflektiert für sie
- session-capture als MCP-Tool freigeben wäre kontraproduktiv
- Cursor braucht keine separate Integration (spricht MCP)

### Was gebaut wurde (bleibt)
- Server: `/api/session-capture` Endpoint (REST, für dumb clients)
- Server: API-Key Auth funktioniert

### Was deaktiviert wurde  
- Stop-Hook in settings.json entfernt
- Hook-Script bleibt als Referenz in ~/code/claude/malte/hooks/auto-capture.py

### Nächster Schritt
- claude-5vo: Agent Closing Instructions in alle Agent-Definitionen einbauen
title: Auto-Capture Hooks für Claude Code, Cursor & shikigami-bot

close_reason: content/narrative semantics documented in dataclass docstrings + MCP tool descriptions; refine.py fallback order confirmed correct; 141/141 tests passing
description:
## Problem
Most memories have empty `content` and all substance in `narrative`. The schema has redundancy and the roles of these fields are unclear. The analysis prompt was showing content[:200] which was always empty.

## Solution
- Document field semantics clearly (narrative = prose description, content = structured/raw data?)
- Consider merging into single field or establishing clear usage guidelines
- Update MCP tool descriptions to clarify when to use which

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Field semantics documented
- [ ] MCP tool descriptions updated
title: Clarify content vs narrative field semantics

description:
## Kontext

Teil 2 der AST-Initiative (nach open-brain-f6r). Waehrend Teil 1 reine In-Session Navigation bietet (wie claude-mem), geht Teil 2 weiter: Symbol-Metadaten werden persistent in Postgres gespeichert und ermöglichen cross-session symbol-aware Retrieval.

## Ziel

Antworten auf Fragen wie:
- "Alle Memories die entstanden sind als ich an authenticate_user gearbeitet habe"
- "Was weiss open-brain ueber die Klasse PostgresDataLayer?"
- "Zeig mir alles was jemals ueber search_memories notiert wurde"

Das ist nicht durch Volltext-Suche loesbar — dafuer braucht man explizite Symbol-Tags.

## Was gebaut wird

### 1. DB-Schema: code_symbols Tabelle

```sql
CREATE TABLE code_symbols (
  id           UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  observation_id UUID REFERENCES observations(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  project      TEXT NOT NULL,
  symbol_name  TEXT NOT NULL,        -- z.B. "authenticate_user"
  symbol_type  TEXT NOT NULL,        -- "function" | "class" | "method"
  file_path    TEXT,
  line_num     INT,
  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX ON code_symbols (project, symbol_name);
```

### 2. PostToolUse-Hook Erweiterung

Wenn PostToolUse einen Read-Tool-Aufruf auf eine .py-Datei erkennt:
1. Python ast-Modul parst die Datei
2. Extrahiert: alle Funktionen/Klassen/Methoden die im gelesenen Bereich liegen
3. Speichert symbol-Eintraege zusammen mit der Observation-ID
4. Dedup: gleicher symbol_name + file_path + observation_id = kein Duplikat

### 3. search_memories API-Erweiterung

Neuer optionaler Parameter symbol_name (oder symbol_filter):
- Gibt Observations zurueck die im Kontext des angegebenen Symbols entstanden sind
- Kombinierbar mit bestehendem Volltext/Vektor-Search

### 4. ob-search Skill Update

ob-search Skill unterstuetzt neuen --symbol Flag:
- Beispiel: "Suche Memories ueber PostgresDataLayer.search_memories"
- Nutzt den neuen symbol_name Filter auf dem Server

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tabelle code_symbols in Postgres angelegt (Migration)
- [ ] PostToolUse-Hook extrahiert Symbole bei Python-File-Reads
- [ ] Dedup: kein doppelter Eintrag bei erneutem Read derselben Datei
- [ ] search_memories Endpoint unterstuetzt symbol_name Filter
- [ ] ob-search Skill (Plugin) nutzt Symbol-Filter
- [ ] Unit-Tests: Extraktion, Dedup, Retrieval per Symbol
- [ ] Integration-Test: File lesen -> Symbol gespeichert -> Retrieval funktioniert

## Means of Compliance

| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | DB-Schema | unit | test_code_symbols_schema() |
| 2 | Hook-Extraktion | unit | test_extract_symbols_on_file_read() |
| 3 | Dedup | unit | test_symbol_dedup() |
| 4 | API-Filter | integ | test_search_memories_by_symbol() |
| 5 | Skill-Update | integ | Manual: ob-search mit symbol-Flag |

## Dependencies
- open-brain-f6r: Python ast-Infrastruktur aus Teil 1 wiederverwenden
- open-brain-ddz: Hooks stabil
title: ob-smart-explore Teil 2: Server-Side Symbol-Index fuer cross-session Retrieval

description:
## Kontext
Das metadata-JSON-Feld ist bereits in der DB vorhanden und wird in _row_to_memory eingelesen. Es wird aber noch nicht über die MCP-Tools geschrieben. Das ist das Fundament für die gesamte Learning Pipeline.

## Was zu tun ist
- SaveMemoryParams: metadata: dict[str, Any] | None = None hinzufügen
- UpdateMemoryParams: metadata: dict[str, Any] | None = None hinzufügen (JSONB-Merge: metadata || $n)
- SearchParams: metadata_filter: dict[str, str] | None = None hinzufügen
- postgres.py save_memory: INSERT um metadata-Feld erweitern
- postgres.py update_memory: JSONB-Merge-Update für metadata
- postgres.py search: metadata_filter in WHERE-Clause auswerten (metadata->>'key' = 'value')
- server.py MCP-Tools: Parameter für save_memory, update_memory, search ergänzen

## Akzeptanzkriterien
- [ ] save_memory(metadata={...}) persistiert JSON in DB
- [ ] update_memory(metadata={...}) merged JSONB (nicht überschreibt)
- [ ] search(metadata_filter={'status': 'open'}) filtert korrekt
- [ ] Alle bestehenden Tests weiterhin grün

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | save_memory metadata | unit | test_save_memory_with_metadata |
| 2 | update_memory JSONB merge | unit | test_update_memory_metadata_merge |
| 3 | search metadata_filter | unit | test_search_metadata_filter |
| 4 | Regression | unit | alle bestehenden Tests grün |

## Relevante Dateien
- python/src/open_brain/data_layer/interface.py
- python/src/open_brain/data_layer/postgres.py
- python/src/open_brain/server.py

description:
Die Implementierung weicht in mehreren Punkten vom Plan (elysium-proxmox/docs/plans/open-brain.md) ab.
Vereinfachungen #1 (metadata JSONB) und #2 (single session_id) sind Verbesserungen und bleiben.

## Korrekturen

### Schema (Migrationen)
- [ ] memories: `subtitle TEXT`, `narrative TEXT` Felder hinzufügen (content bleibt als Ersatz für text)
- [ ] memories: `access_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0`, `last_accessed_at TIMESTAMPTZ` hinzufügen
- [ ] memories: Stored TSVECTOR mit 4-Level Gewichtung (title=A, subtitle=B, narrative=C, content=D) + GIN Index
- [ ] session_summaries: strukturierte Felder (request, investigated, learned, completed, next_steps, files_read, files_edited, notes, prompt_number)
- [ ] sessions: `status TEXT DEFAULT 'active' CHECK (...)`, `prompt_counter INTEGER DEFAULT 0`
- [ ] memory_relationships: CHECK constraint auf 8 relation_types, `confidence` statt `weight`

### Hybrid Search
- [ ] hybrid_search() Return anpassen: subtitle zurückgeben, score statt rrf_score
- [ ] websearch_to_tsquery statt plainto_tsquery
- [ ] Stored TSVECTOR nutzen statt dynamischer Berechnung

### Priority System
- [ ] update_priority() Funktion mit 3-Faktor-Formel (0.4 recency + 0.4 importance + 0.2 usage)
- [ ] logUsage() in postgres.ts: update_priority() aufrufen statt +0.02 Boost
- [ ] decay_unused_priorities() kann bleiben als Ergänzung

### DataLayer / TypeScript
- [ ] DataLayer Interface + postgres.ts an neue Felder anpassen
- [ ] save_memory: subtitle, narrative akzeptieren
- [ ] session_summaries: strukturierte Felder speichern/lesen

## Means of Compliance

| 2 | Stored TSVECTOR + GIN Index aktiv | integ | \d memories zeigt search_vector + idx |

| 5 | CHECK constraints auf relation_type | integ | INSERT mit ungültigem Typ schlägt fehl |
| 6 | TypeScript kompiliert fehlerfrei | unit | npx tsc --noEmit |

close_reason: reranker.py implemented, RERANK_ENABLED/RERANK_MODEL config added, search+search_by_concept fetch 3x and rerank; 225 unit tests passing (1 integration test with real API call)
description:
## Context
open-brain search uses hybrid retrieval (pgvector cosine + tsvector FTS via RRF). RRF is statistical fusion — it doesn't understand query-document relevance semantically. Voyage Rerank-2.5 can act as a second-pass quality gate.

## Approach
- In `search` and `search_by_concept`: fetch 3x requested limit from hybrid search, then rerank with Voyage Rerank-2.5, return top N
- Transparent to MCP callers — results just get more relevant
- Rerank-2.5 supports instruction-following: can pass natural language steering (e.g. "prefer decision memories over logs")
- Add config flag `RERANK_ENABLED` (default: true) + `RERANK_MODEL` (default: rerank-2.5)
- New module `python/src/open_brain/data_layer/reranker.py`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Reranker module calls Voyage Rerank API with query + candidate documents
- [ ] `search` fetches 3x limit, reranks, returns top N when reranking enabled
- [ ] `search_by_concept` uses reranking similarly
- [ ] Config flag to disable reranking (for latency-sensitive use or debugging)
- [ ] Unit tests for reranker module (mocked API)
- [ ] Integration test verifying reranked results differ from raw RRF order

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | Reranker module | unit | test_reranker.py (mocked API) |
| 2 | search uses reranking | unit | test_search_rerank.py |
| 3 | search_by_concept uses reranking | unit | test_search_by_concept_rerank.py |
| 4 | Config flag | unit | test with RERANK_ENABLED=false |
| 5 | Reranked results differ from raw | integ | test with real API call |

## Non-goals
- Reranking for auto-linking (background, precision less critical)
- Reranking for refine_memories duplicate detection (needs pair scoring, not reranking)
title: Add Voyage Rerank-2.5 as second-pass reranker for search

close_reason: 3 Plugin-Skills (ob-smart-search, ob-smart-outline, ob-smart-unfold) + 3 Python scripts + 28/28 Unit-Tests passing; Python ast-native, tree-sitter optional mit graceful fallback; CommandRunner DI Pattern; kein Server-Endpoint
description:
## Was claude-mem wirklich macht (Research open-brain-yju)

claude-mem's AST-Feature ist **kein Capture-Improvement** — es sind drei MCP-Tools für token-effiziente Codebase-Navigation:

| Tool | Zweck | Token-Kosten |
|------|-------|-------------|
| smart_search | Symbole über alle Dateien finden (ranked) | ~2-6k |
| smart_outline | Strukturskelett einer Datei (Klassen, Funktionen) | ~1-2k |
| smart_unfold | Vollständiger Code eines Symbols (AST-bounded) | ~0.4-2k |

**Wie claude-mem es implementiert:**
- MCP-Tools, nicht Hooks oder Skills
- Tree-sitter CLI via subprocess (keine Native Bindings)
- Kein Persistieren von AST-Daten — alles on-demand, in-session
- Ergebnis: 4-18x Token-Einsparnis gegenüber vollem Dateilesen

**Warum das für open-brain interessant ist:**
Wir können weiter gehen als claude-mem: AST-Metadaten persistieren (cross-session), damit Retrieval nicht nur schneller ist sondern symbol-aware ("alle Memories über search_memories").

---

## Was wir bauen wollen

### Teil 1: Plugin-Skills (claude-mem-Parität, Python-native)

Drei neue Skills im open-brain Plugin, analog zu claude-mem aber:
- Python's built-in ast-Modul statt tree-sitter (für .py Dateien)
- tree-sitter CLI für JS/TS/Go/Rust (optional, graceful fallback)
- Als Plugin-Skills (SKILL.md + script), nicht MCP-Server-Tools

**ob-smart-search**: Symbol über alle Dateien eines Projekts finden
- Input: query, path, file_pattern, max_results
- Parst alle .py Dateien mit Python ast, .ts/.js mit tree-sitter CLI (wenn vorhanden)
- Ranking: exact match > prefix > substring
- Output: Datei + Zeile + Signatur + umgebender Kontext

**ob-smart-outline**: Strukturskelett einer Datei
- Input: file_path
- Output: alle Klassen/Funktionen/Imports mit Zeilen (keine Implementierungen)
- 70-80% kürzer als volle Datei

**ob-smart-unfold**: Vollständiger Code eines Symbols
- Input: file_path, symbol_name
- AST-bounded: garantiert vollständige Funktion/Klasse ohne manuellen scroll
- Fallback: grep-basiert wenn kein AST-Parser verfügbar

### Teil 2: Server-Side Symbol-Index (optional, separates Bead)

Erweiterung des open-brain Servers um persistente Symbol-Metadaten:
- Neue Tabelle code_symbols: observation_id, symbol_name, symbol_type, file_path, line_num
- PostToolUse-Hook: bei File-Read .py-Datei parsen, Symbols extrahieren, mit Observation speichern
- Neuer Filter in search_memories: symbol_name=XYZ — gibt Memories die im Kontext dieses Symbols entstanden sind
- Enabler für: "Was weiss open-brain ueber die Funktion authenticate_user?"

Entscheidung: Teil 2 als eigenes Bead nach Teil 1 stabil.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Teil 1: Plugin-Skills
- [ ] ob-smart-search Skill: findet Python-Symbole via ast-Modul, optional JS/TS via tree-sitter CLI
- [ ] ob-smart-outline Skill: gibt Strukturskelett zurück (Klassen, Methoden, Imports, Zeilen)
- [ ] ob-smart-unfold Skill: extrahiert vollständiges Symbol, AST-bounded
- [ ] Graceful fallback wenn tree-sitter nicht installiert (Python-only Modus)
- [ ] Kein Server-Endpoint nötig für Teil 1 — reine Dateianalyse lokal im Plugin
- [ ] Unit-Tests: AST-Extraktion Python, Ranking-Logik, Fallback-Pfad

### Teil 2 (separates Bead, hier nur Spec-Platzhalter)
- [ ] Schema: code_symbols Tabelle in Postgres
- [ ] PostToolUse-Hook erweitert: Symbol-Extraktion bei File-Reads
- [ ] search_memories unterstuetzt symbol_name Filter

## Dependencies
- open-brain-ddz: Plugin-Hooks muessen stabil sein bevor wir erweitern
- Python ast: built-in, keine neue Dep
- tree-sitter CLI: optionale Dep (npm install -g tree-sitter-cli + grammars)

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | ob-smart-search | unit | test_smart_search_python(), test_smart_search_fallback() |
| 2 | ob-smart-outline | unit | test_smart_outline_classes_functions() |
| 3 | ob-smart-unfold | unit | test_smart_unfold_exact(), test_smart_unfold_fallback() |
| 4 | Graceful fallback | unit | test_no_treesitter_installed() |
| 5 | Integration | integ | Manual: smart_search auf open-brain Python-Codebase |
notes: Break analysis (2026-03-12): Skills are pure SKILL.md + Python scripts pattern. ob-search/ob-triage are AI instruction files only. New skills need: (1) SKILL.md in plugin/skills/ob-smart-{search,outline,unfold}/, (2) Python scripts in plugin/scripts/smart_{search,outline,unfold}.py, (3) Tests in python/tests/test_smart_*.py. Python ast built-in = no risk. tree-sitter CLI optional = graceful fallback required. CommandRunner DI pattern needed for subprocess calls. No integration risks — pure local file analysis, no server deps.
title: ob-smart-explore: Plugin-Skills für AST-basierte Code-Navigation (Teil 1)

close_reason: SKILL.md Step 4 updated: Promote → status:materialized+materialized_to, Archive/Delete/Merge → status:discarded+discard_reason
description:
## Kontext
Wenn ob-triage ein Learning promoted oder discarded, muss der Status in open-brain aktualisiert werden. Aktuell passiert das nicht — nach dem Promote ist das Learning nicht als 'materialized' markiert.

## Was zu tun ist
Im ob-triage SKILL.md, Schritt 4 'Execute Approved Actions' ergänzen:
- Nach Promote: update_memory(id=..., metadata={'status': 'materialized', 'materialized_to': '<ziel>'})
- Nach Archive/Discard: update_memory(id=..., metadata={'status': 'discarded', 'discard_reason': '<grund>'})
- Nach Merge: überlebendes Memory updated, gelöschtes bekommt status: 'discarded'

Außerdem: ob-triage kann type:learning bereits filtern. Nach diesem Bead gilt:
- /ob-triage type:learning → zeigt alle Learnings
- Status 'open' erkennbar via metadata.status

## Akzeptanzkriterien
- [ ] SKILL.md: Nach Promote wird update_memory mit status:materialized aufgerufen
- [ ] SKILL.md: Nach Discard/Archive wird update_memory mit status:discarded aufgerufen
- [ ] SKILL.md: materialized_to-Feld wird mit Ziel befüllt

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Promote-Tracking | review | SKILL.md Schritt 4 zeigt update_memory-Aufruf |
| 2 | Discard-Tracking | review | SKILL.md zeigt status:discarded Update |
| 3 | materialized_to | review | Ziel-Feld im Update-Aufruf dokumentiert |

## Relevante Dateien
- /Users/malte/code/open-brain/plugin/skills/ob-triage/SKILL.md
title: [ob-triage] Status-Tracking nach Promote/Discard

close_reason: Triage pipeline implemented: triage_memories + materialize_memories + run_lifecycle_pipeline MCP tools, learning/session_summary/observation type differentiation, 28 new tests, 253 total passing
description:
## Context
Raw observations from the plugin accumulate during sessions. They need a lifecycle:
collect → refine → triage → materialize into actionable outcomes.

open-brain already has refine_memories (merge/deduplicate). This extends the pipeline
with triage (classify what action a memory requires) and materialization (execute that action).

## Inspired By
- MIRA Feedback Ground Truth Loop (doc 17): 4-layer knowledge system
- claude-mem refinement pipeline: dedup + vector similarity consolidation
- learnings-pipeline skill: extract → review → materialize

## Knowledge Layers (from MIRA, generalized)
1. **Data** (statistical) → auto-adjust confidence/priority
2. **Memory** (associative) → keep in open-brain, refine over time
3. **Standards** (deterministic) → materialize to CLAUDE.md / standards files
4. **Code** (structural) → create bead for code change

## Triage Decision
After refinement, each memory gets classified:
- **keep** → stays in open-brain as-is
- **merge** → combine with related memories (existing refine_memories)
- **promote** → materialize to standard/instruction (new)
- **scaffold** → create bead for code change (new)
- **archive** → low-value, deprioritize/remove

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Triage classification added to refine_memories or as separate tool
- [ ] Materialization actions for promote (→ standards) and scaffold (→ bead)
- [ ] Pipeline can run periodically (manual trigger or scheduled)
- [ ] Differentiation between observation, session_summary, and learning types

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | Triage classification | unit | test_triage_classification() |
| 2 | Materialization | integ | promote → file written, scaffold → bead created |
| 3 | Pipeline trigger | demo | Manual run produces triage report |
| 4 | Type differentiation | unit | test_memory_types() |

## Dependencies
- Depends on open-brain-187 (plugin must capture observations first)
title: Memory Lifecycle Pipeline: collect → refine → triage → materialize

close_reason: open-brain running as Docker container on services_default network, health check passing, deploy.sh uses docker compose + op run, systemd unit disabled
description:
## Context
open-brain currently runs as a bare systemd unit (uv run python -m open_brain) on LXC 116 (SSH host: services).
We now have a Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml. Migrate the app to run as a Docker container.

## Current state (verified 2026-03-09)
- Docker 29.2.1 already running on services ✓
- Postgres already a Docker container: pgvector/pgvector:pg18, named 'elysium-postgres', port 127.0.0.1:5432 ✓
- pgvector already installed — NO migration needed ✓
- open-brain data already in 'services_pgdata' Docker volume ✓
- Only open-brain app itself is not yet Dockerized

## Scope
1. Build open-brain Docker image on services
2. Add open-brain to the existing Docker Compose setup (alongside elysium-postgres + langfuse)
3. Connect open-brain container to the existing elysium-postgres container (same Docker network)
4. Mount /opt/open-brain/clients.json into container
5. Keep 1Password: op run --env-file=.env.tpl -- docker compose up -d
6. Update deploy.sh: git pull + docker compose up --build -d (remove uv sync + systemctl restart)
7. Disable/retire old systemd unit open-brain.service

## Key design
- Use docker-compose.service.yml (app only, no postgres)
- DATABASE_URL must reference the container name, not localhost: postgresql://...@elysium-postgres:5432/open_brain
- Ensure both containers are on the same Docker network

## No data migration needed
- All data is in existing services_pgdata volume, stays there
- pgvector already installed in elysium-postgres image

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] open-brain runs as Docker container on services
- [ ] Connects to existing elysium-postgres container (same network)
- [ ] deploy.sh uses docker compose (not systemctl)
- [ ] clients.json mounted correctly
- [ ] Health check passes: ssh services 'curl http://localhost:8091/health'
- [ ] Old systemd unit disabled
title: Deploy open-brain via Docker Compose on services (LXC 116)

close_reason: Decision: plugin-side skill (AST at capture time), not server-side indexing. Follow-up: open-brain-f6r (ob-smart-capture), blocked on ddz.
description:
## Context
claude-mem v10.5.2 added tree-sitter-based AST parsing (smart-explore) for structure-aware code reading. Evaluate adopting this for open-brain to create symbol-level memory indexing.

## Research Findings
- **smart_search**: Find symbols across codebase via AST
- **smart_outline**: Structural skeleton of a file (functions, classes, imports)
- **smart_unfold**: Extract specific symbol by AST node boundaries
- **4-8x token savings** vs reading full files
- Uses tree-sitter with grammars for JS/TS/Python/Go/Rust/etc.
- Currently a skill in claude-mem plugin, not a memory storage feature

## Evaluation for open-brain
- **Symbol-level indexing**: Store memories tagged with function/class context
- **Better observation quality**: AST-aware extraction feeds richer data into observations
- **Precise retrieval**: "memories about function X" vs "mentions of X in any file"
- **Challenge**: Tree-sitter Python bindings less mature than JS, multi-language deps

## Decision
This is an **enhancement to the plugin** (open-brain-187), not a standalone feature.
Can be implemented as a skill bundled with the plugin, or as server-side indexing.
Priority: after plugin MVP is working.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Research documented (done — see above)
- [ ] Decision on approach: plugin-side skill vs server-side indexing
- [ ] Follow-up bead created if worth adopting

## Means of Compliance
| # | AK | MoC | Nachweis |
|---|---|-----|---------|
| 1 | Research complete | doc | Findings in description above |
| 2 | Decision documented | doc | Approach chosen, rationale in notes |
| 3 | Follow-up beads | review | Linked beads if applicable |
notes:
## Decision (2026-03-10)

**Chosen approach: Plugin-side skill (AST at capture time)**

Rationale:
- Server stays clean — no tree-sitter Python bindings on LXC 116
- Plugin MVP (187) is working — adding a skill is a natural extension
- The primary benefit is at capture time: richer observations → better pgvector retrieval
- Server-side indexing (alternative) adds schema complexity without proportional gain — pgvector already handles semantic search
- Plugin skills are opt-in: users who don't need AST-aware capture skip tree-sitter install

Implementation sketch:
- New plugin skill: ob-smart-capture (or extend hook_runner.py)
- On PostToolUse: if tool reads a .py/.ts/.go file, use tree-sitter to extract symbol context (function/class boundaries) before saving observation
- Observation gets tagged with  metadata
- ob-search can then filter by symbol tag (new optional field on server)

Priority: after DDZ migration (open-brain-ddz) is stable. Plugin hooks are already in use — AST enhancement is additive.
title: Research claude-mem AST memory enhancement for open-brain

After merging bead/3od/impl to main, manually verify the Python MCP server works end-to-end.

Test steps:
1. cd python/ && uv run pytest -m 'integration' (with valid VOYAGE_API_KEY and DATABASE_URL)
2. Start server: uv run python -m open_brain.server
3. Connect an MCP client and test all 8 tools: search, timeline, get_observations, save_memory, search_by_concept, get_context, stats, refine_memories
4. Verify OAuth 2.1 flow works (authorize -> code exchange -> access token)
5. Verify hybrid search returns results (pgvector + FTS + RRF)
6. Confirm TS code can be safely removed after verification
title: Verify Python MCP server end-to-end after merge of bead/3od/impl
